text.title: Token Rules, Antipatterns, and Agentic Workflow
text.summary: Naming rules, contrast-pairing rule, depth limit, known antipatterns, and agentic workflow guidance for design token systems.

section.title: Core Rules

rule.statement: Name semantic tokens by purpose, not appearance. Use color.text.muted, not color.gray.600.
rule.rationale: Appearance-based names make theming impossible — color.gray.600 cannot be a dark-mode override because the name describes the light-mode hue. Purpose-based names survive theme changes because the purpose stays constant.

rule.statement: Pair every semantic foreground token with its background token. Lint every text/background pair for WCAG 2.2 AA contrast (4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text) in CI.
rule.rationale: Contrast violations are the most common accessibility failure in design systems. Catching them at token-generation time costs seconds; catching them in a11y audit costs sprints.

rule.statement: Limit token nesting depth to four levels maximum (e.g., color.button.primary.hover). Enforce in CI.
rule.rationale: LLMs generate tokens with 6–8 nesting levels (color.button.primary.hover.background.disabled). At that depth, naming collapses — no human can find any token in a library of 1,500+ without semantic search.

rule.statement: Treat token rename as a breaking change. Version token packages with SemVer. Ship a migration codemod with every rename.
rule.rationale: Consumers depend on token name stability. A silent rename of color.text.default to color.text.primary breaks every downstream consumer silently — CSS variables go undefined, not errored.

section.reference: CI script: fail if semantic text/background pair < 4.5:1

antipattern.description: Primitive tokens leaking into component code (e.g., color.blue.500 used in a button component directly).
antipattern.reason: Makes theming and dark mode impossible. Any change to blue.500 breaks every component referencing it unpredictably.

antipattern.description: LLM-generated tokens skipping the semantic layer entirely — outputting only primitive and component tiers.
antipattern.reason: Without semantic layer, theming requires editing every component token. Force 3-tier output schema in the prompt.

antipattern.description: Auto-deriving dark theme algorithmically from light theme.
antipattern.reason: Algorithmic inversion produces poor contrast and violates accessibility requirements. Dark-mode semantic layer must be hand-tuned and contrast-validated.

antipattern.description: Cross-platform output emitting px values for iOS (expects pt) or Android (expects dp/sp).
antipattern.reason: LLMs paste px everywhere without transforms. Style Dictionary must declare unit transforms per platform or assets render at wrong sizes on mobile.

section.title: Agentic Workflow
section.p: Agents can: (a) generate initial 3-tier token JSON from a design brief, (b) lint token files for naming and reference-cycle violations, (c) generate platform outputs (CSS variables, iOS/Android resources, Tailwind config) via Style Dictionary, (d) audit existing CSS for hardcoded values to tokenize. Human review for naming and the semantic layer is mandatory — names outlive the codebase.

Gotchas: W3C DTCG schema uses $value, not value — agents may emit obsolete shape. Hex/OKLCH conversions produce subtly off colors silently — assert exact round-trip match. Generated dark themes need manual contrast verification — never accept agent-declared "accessible".
